Title: Scrap RM3.5mil MP allocation to boost savings, says Pas leader
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A Pas leader has proposed that the government abolish the special allocation of RM3.5 million distributed to each government member of parliament as a cost-saving measure amid ongoing tensions in the Middle East.
The party's Information chief Ahmad Fadhli Shaari said if such a step were implemented, it would result in far greater savings compared with the proposal to reduce MPs' petrol allowances of RM1,500.
"The savings would be greater if the RM3.5 million special allocation is abolished.
"The government can continue to assist the people in government parliamentary constituencies in the same way it assists those in opposition constituencies.
"Wouldn't that be more effective? Savings of around half a billion ringgit a year.
"I am confident the government can agree to this.
"Then, if they also wish to abolish the petrol allowance, that is up to them," he said on his Facebook page today.
He was commenting on a report citing the Malaysian Youth Council (MBM), which put forward four key proposals to the government as measures to balance the impact of global pressures arising from Middle East tensions, including reviewing the petrol allowances of all 222 MPs.
MBM secretary-general Ahmad Farhan Rosli said the move to review and reduce the petrol allowances of all Dewan Rakyat members could be seen as a symbol of leading by example and national solidarity.
MBM also recommended that the government expand the BUDI diesel subsidy and relax the conditions for private vehicle owners, with a reasonable quota of around 300 litres per month, as well as accelerate efforts to diversify the country's energy sources by exploring alternative supplies outside Gulf countries.
Fadhli, who is also the Pasir Mas MP, said he personally had no issue if the government wished to implement the proposal to reduce petrol allowances.
"But the savings obtained would not be all that significant, RM1,500 multiplied by 222 MPs, and even then, after deducting Cabinet members who do not receive petrol allowance entitlements, how much would the monthly savings actually be?" he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Brain drain worsens as trainee doctors turn to Singapore
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The country's public healthcare sector is facing a critical workforce crisis following the mass rejection of housemanship placements by trainee doctors.
A research note by MBSB Research revealed that of the 5,000 housemanship placements offered by the Health Ministry in January 2026 to absorb the surplus of graduates, only 529 individuals, or roughly 10.5 per cent, reported for duty.
The situation reflected an escalating talent drain, particularly as neighbouring Singapore adjusted its strategy to attract medical specialists from Malaysia.
The research report highlighted that recruitment agents from the republic are now targeting final-year medical students directly, without waiting for them to gain prior work experience.
"Recruitment agents are reported to approach students at top local universities before they sit their final Bachelor of Medicine and Bachelor of Surgery (MBBS) examinations," the research note said.
Among the institutions targeted were Universiti Malaya, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia and Universiti Sains Malaysia (USM).
USM became a focus after it received recognition from the Singapore Medical Council in February, increasing the pool of eligible Malaysian talent by 30 to 40 per cent.
In terms of remuneration, Singapore's offers are far more competitive, with starting salaries reaching S$110,000 (approximately RM385,000) per year, five to six times higher than entry-level offers from the Health Ministry in Malaysia.
Singapore provides permanent employment status and pension benefits, compared with Malaysia's system, which still relied on contract appointments without long-term career guarantees.
It is understood that medical students are now more inclined to sign employment contracts in Singapore immediately upon graduation to avoid the uncertainty associated with housemanship placements in Malaysia.
To address the issue, the Health Ministry is reportedly developing a new formula, expected to be announced by the end of 2026, which included ending the contract doctor system and restoring permanent appointments as the standard for new graduates.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: More US naval forces arrive in Middle East
Article:
WASHINGTON: More US sailors and marines have arrived in West Asia aboard the USS Tripoli, an amphibious assault ship, the US military said on Saturday, reported German Press Agency (dpa).
The USS Tripoli, a flagship for a combined naval force of some 3,500 personnel arrived in the region on Friday, the US Central Command (CENTCOM), the military's regional command responsible for the area, wrote on X.
The forces are joining thousands of US marines already deployed to Middle East as part of a massive military build-up ahead of the Iran war.
In total, the US has deployed over 50,000 troops to the region, according to earlier statements.
The US and Israel launched joint attacks on Iran on Feb 28.
Teheran has responded by striking Israel and Gulf states hosting US military facilities, while also effectively blocking the Strait of Hormuz, a key waterway for global oil shipments.
Since the start of the war, US forces have struck more than 11,000 targets in Iran and damaged or destroyed more than 150 Iranian vessels, CENTCOM said in a separate post later on Saturday.
– Bernama-dpa
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Police detain two PKR men over alleged extortion in Wangsa Maju
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Two men who allegedly extorted a massage parlour with "protection money" in Wangsa Maju were detained by the police.
City police chief Datuk Fadil Marsus confirmed the arrests, Malaysiakini reported.
The New Straits Times has reached out to Datuk Fadil for confirmation and additional information on the arrests.
On Friday, Datuk Fadil said police were investigating the case after receiving three reports regarding the matter.
He said the case was being investigated under Section 385 of the Penal Code for putting a person in fear of injury to commit extortion, which carries up to seven years' imprisonment, a fine, or whipping, or any two of these punishments.
The case was also investigated under Section 233 of the Communications and Multimedia Act for improper use of network facilities, which carries a fine of up to RM50,000, imprisonment up to one year, or both.
The investigation followed footage allegedly showing the two men demanding "protection money" from the premises.
Federal Territories PKR leadership council chairman Datuk Azman Abidin said in a statement that the men, who are members of the Federal Territories Residents' Representative Council, should resign following the allegations.
He said their resignations are necessary to prevent any interference with police investigations and the party.
On Thursday, Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Federal Territories) Hannah Yeoh directed that a police report be lodged over the matter.
She said the video had raised public concern and warranted immediate investigation, adding that there would be zero tolerance for any form of protection money or middleman practices.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mount Semeru erupts nine times today, triggers pyroclastic flows
Article:
JAKARTA: Mount Semeru in East Java erupted nine times today, with the latest eruption sending an ash column up to 800 metres above its summit and triggering pyroclastic flows that travelled 3.5 kilometres down its southeastern slopes.
Mount Semeru Observation Post officer Mukdas Sofian said the volcano, the highest on Java Island, has been erupting since 12.21am, with the ninth eruption recorded at 8.49am.
"The ash column at 8.49am was observed to be white to grey with thick intensity drifting toward the southwest, while seismic recordings showed a maximum amplitude of 22mm and a duration of about four minutes and six seconds," he said, according to ANTARA News Agency, today.
Mukdas said the strongest eruption occurred at 1.01am, producing an ash column reaching approximately 1,000 metres above the summit, with moderate intensity drifting southwest.
Authorities said Mount Semeru remains at Level III (Alert) status.
Residents are advised not to carry out any activities within 13km in the southeast sector along Besuk Kobokan.
They are also prohibited from being within 500 metres of riverbanks along the same area due to the risk of pyroclastic flow expansion and lahar flows reaching up to 17km from the summit.
In addition, the public is barred from activities within a five km radius of the crater due to the danger of incandescent rock ejections. – BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Sultan crosses paths with accident victim he once helped
Article:
KUANTAN: The Sultan of Pahang, known for his caring nature in assisting accident victims while travelling, yesterday crossed paths with a person he had helped following a crash on the East Coast Highway earlier this year.
The unexpected reunion took place during the Bera parliamentary constituency and Koperasi Serbausaha Makmur Bhd Aidilfitri open house celebration in Bera, where the motorcyclist, identified as Faris Farhan, met Al-Sultan Abdullah Ri'ayatuddin Al-Mustafa Billah Shah.
Recounting the incident, His Royal Highness, who later addressed the crowd, said he had come across an accident victim whom he had assisted previously.
"His mother or sister lives in Bera. His name is Faris Farhan… he appears healthy, but both his hands are still bandaged.
"I remember that after the accident, I lifted him (Faris) into the police car and sent him to the hospital.
"During the incident, he was riding a motorcycle and hit a car that had stopped on the road shoulder.
"The car from Terengganu had stopped as it had run out of petrol," he said, as guests listened attentively at the Bera District Council hall.
The ruler drew laughter from the crowd when he said that he had not only helped send Faris to the hospital but had also assisted the car owner in making arrangements to send the individual to the police station to lodge a report.
"I do not know who was right or wrong in the accident, but when we see someone involved in an accident, we should stop and step out to help, as it is 'fardu kifayah' (a communal responsibility) to assist those in need," he said.
Al-Sultan Abdullah had
stopped to assist Faris
while travelling from Kuala Lumpur to Kuantan on Jan 23 this year.
Al-Sultan Abdullah's willingness to step out of his car to help accident victims reflects his unwavering commitment to the wellbeing of the people, something he has consistently demonstrated, even during his tenure as the Yang di-Pertuan Agong.
Several videos and images of Al-Sultan Abdullah assisting accident victims, checking on their condition and arranging help, including ambulances, have gone viral on social media.
Meanwhile, Al-Sultan Abdullah said hosting and attending open house events should be done with good intentions.
"If we organise or attend open houses to strengthen ukhuwah (brotherhood), and not to discredit others, it becomes an act of worship.
"I thank leaders at every level in this state who have made Pahang the most harmonious state.
"As the Sultan of Pahang,
harmony
is what I seek, and I hope it will continue to be preserved," he said.
Also present yesterday were the Tengku Ampuan of Pahang, Tunku Azizah Aminah Maimunah Iskandariah; the Tengku Mahkota of Pahang, Tengku Hassanal Ibrahim Alam Shah Al-Sultan Abdullah; and Bera member of parliament Datuk Seri Ismail Sabri Yaakob.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Petronas denies Philippines fuel supply deal
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Petroliam Nasional Bhd (Petronas) has denied any involvement in a proposed fuel supply arrangement in the Philippines.
The national oil company said it is not aware of, nor has it entered into, any related agreement or commitment.
"Petronas' immediate focus is to ensure a reliable and continuous fuel supply for Malaysia.
"This remains its utmost priority, supported by established supply networks and close coordination with the government," it said in a statement.
Philippine media reported on Friday that Davao del Norte province in Mindanao plans to import 44 million litres of crude oil and gasoline from Malaysia to curb rising oil prices.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ex-soldier describes close encounter with death while hunting communists
Article:
BACHOK: The sound of gunfire, followed by the crashing of the lorry that he was riding in and the sight of his comrades' bodies strewn all over the scene have haunted Mohd Yusof Shaharum for more than 50 years.
Now 77, the father of six still remembers the day — May 16, 1972 — when he narrowly escaped death during an ambush by communist insurgents in the forest of Mong Gajah, Kedah.
Yusof began his service as a lance corporal with the 11th Battalion Police Field Force in 1969.
His unit had been deployed under Ops Gubir, tasked with tracking down insurgents believed to be operating deep in the jungle.
"We were in a platoon under the officer commanding (OC) company. As we moved in, we suddenly heard gunfire," he recalled.
Yusof said he was travelling in one of the military lorries transporting personnel through the rugged terrain.
"Our convoy came under attack. Everyone was on alert. Then suddenly, the lorry lost control while trying to avoid a ravine. It skidded and crashed down a slope," he said.
The impact proved fatal for three personnel in Yusof's vehicle. Amid the chaos, a hand grenade landed nearby and exploded, adding to the carnage.
"The communists were on higher ground, on the hill. They had the advantage," he said.
Yusof was flung out of the lorry when it crashed.
"I tried to grab my weapon, but I felt a burning pain in my leg," he said, recalling how he had been shot in the left thigh.
As he lay injured, he said he saw three communist insurgents coming down the hill.
"They picked me up and threw me onto a pile of bodies," he said.
What left a lasting impression on him was the voice he heard issuing instructions.
"The one giving orders was a woman. She spoke in Chinese. I still remember it clearly." he said.
Besides the gunshot wound on his left thigh, Yusof also suffered other injuries, including wounds to the left side of his head and right elbow.
The insurgents eventually left him at the scene and he survived.  A rescue team later found and evacuated him to Penang for treatment.
Recalling the moments before the ambush, Yusof said there had been some confusion over the instructions that his team received.
"We were told to standby earlier, then later instructed to move out to collect rations. After that, we were told again to go on patrol," he said.
He added that an armoured vehicle was supposed to accompany the convoy that day, but it was not present.
"Even so, our OC decided to proceed," he said.
In a twist of fate, Yusof decided to change his sitting position in the lorry.
"I normally sat in the back of the lorry, but that day, I decided to move to the third seat," he added.
It was a decision that probably saved his life.
"The three who died were sitting at the back," he added.
Yusof continued serving until his retirement on Nov 1, 1994. However, none of his six children followed in his footsteps.
"They chose their own paths. For me, what matters is that they are safe," he said.
Though decades have passed, the scars — both physical and emotional — remain, along with memories of a day that forever changed his life and the lives of those who served beside him.
Yusof was sharing his experience with the press at his home in Kampung Kubang Kawah near Perupok yesterday during a visit by Bachok police chief Superintendent Mohamad Ismail Jamaluddin.
Ismail was visiting Yusof and other veterans in conjunction with the 219th Police Day celebrations.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Terengganu schools urged to provide sufficient drinking water amid hot weather
Article:
KUALA NERUS: The Terengganu State Education Department (JPNT) encouraged all students to bring sufficient drinking water amid the ongoing hot weather.
Its director Kamsah @ Kamal Mohamed said the department is also reminding all principals and headteachers to consistently advise students to stay hydrated to avoid dehydration.
He said checks conducted that day found students had brought their own drinking water, while all schools had been instructed to provide adequate beverages for their pupils.
"We have also held discussions with dining hall operators at boarding schools to ensure enough drinking water is available. We believe schools have facilities such as water filtration systems to support this. This is to ensure students have access to drinking water at all times, especially in boarding schools," he said during a visit to Sekolah Kebangsaan Bukit Tumbuh.
On preparations to cope with the hot weather, he said the department remained on high alert and had taken early measures, particularly during the final week of Ramadan, by reminding all principals and headteachers across 507 schools, involving more than 300,000 students in the state, to implement necessary precautions.
"Every morning and evening, the department is in contact with the Health Department to obtain the latest information on weather conditions and to receive advice for schools if there are any symptoms or signs of heat stroke," he added.
Kamsah said schools have also been instructed to provide water stations at designated locations to make it easier for students to access drinking water nearby.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Two arrested over murder after burnt body found at Kulim plantation
Article:
KULIM: Police have arrested two men to assist in a murder investigation following the discovery of a burnt body at a plantation here yesterday.
The body was found by a member of the public who was bird hunting at Ladang Anak Kulim. The authorities were alerted at 2.25pm.
Kulim police chief Superintendent Zulkifli Azizan said the witness spotted the body from a hilly area.
"Investigations at the scene found that the body was burnt and decomposing.
"A motorcycle found near the remains was also completely charred," he said today.
Zulkifli said police had tracked down the victim's next-of-kin based on the motorcycle's details.
"Family members informed us that a 77-year-old relative had been missing for four days after failing to return home.
"While identification has been made via the motorcycle, we will conduct DNA testing to confirm the identity of the victim."
Police arrested two men, aged 22 and 68, at 1am today. The suspects have been remanded for four days until April 1.
The case is being investigated under Section 302 of the Penal Code for murder.
Police urged anyone with information on the incident to come forward to assist in the investigation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Family of eight escapes as fire destroys home in Melaka
Article:
MELAKA: A family of eight narrowly escaped injury after a fire destroyed most of their home in Kampung Duyong last night.
The blaze, which broke out at 10.12pm, gutted around 80 per cent of the single-storey house, according to the Melaka Fire and Rescue Department.
Two teenage boys, aged 16 and 19, were the only occupants at the time.
One was in a bedroom while the other was in the bathroom when they heard a small explosion and discovered the living room engulfed in flames.
"They ran out through the kitchen with nothing… one of them was only in a towel," said Noridayu Zainal, 35, whose family owns the house.
She said she had been out visiting relatives in Jasin with her mother, 69-year-old Fatimah Ahmat, and other family members when they received an emergency call.
"We rushed home, but almost the entire house had already been destroyed," she said.
The house, inherited from her late father, had been home to the family for more than four decades.
"Everything was gone in an instant… our belongings, our memories," she added.
Noridayu said the family is now staying temporarily at her brother's home in Durian Tunggal.
Firefighters received an emergency call at 10.19pm and dispatched crews from Bukit Katil and Padang Temu stations.
The fire was brought under control at 11.32pm and fully extinguished by 12.30am.
A total of 13 personnel were involved in the operation, and no injuries were reported. The cause of the fire and the extent of losses are under investigation.
Meanwhile, Duyong assemblyman Mohd Noor Helmy Abdul Halem visited the family and said assistance would be coordinated with relevant agencies.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Japan seeks to expand investment in Malaysia's digital, high-tech sectors
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Japan is aiming to expand its investment in Malaysia's digital industry, encompassing niche areas such as high-tech chip products and data centres.
Masuo Kuremura, Special Advisor to the Minister for Regional Head, Asia-Pacific at Japan's Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry (METI), said there are plans to increase investment in Malaysia for more advanced new applications using Japanese technology.
"We will promote collaboration between Japanese and Malaysian companies, utilising Japanese technology in the digital industry for applications in artificial intelligence (AI), biotechnology, and food sectors to enhance investment," he told Bernama during the Malaysia-Japan Fast Track Pitch, here recently.
Kuremura said that investment in the retail space from Japan to Malaysia is also anticipated to increase in 2026.
"Malaysia has a pivotal role in the supply chain for Japan based on its manufacturing capability in semiconductor and chip products and the automotive industries.
"One example is the established collaboration between Perodua and Daihatsu in the manufacturing sector that has enabled Malaysia to be a good supplier in the automotive industry," he said.
Kuremura highlighted that Japanese companies are eager to continue providing innovative solutions to enhance Malaysia's manufacturing capacity within the global supply chain, positioning themselves as suppliers of premium products.
Major Japanese semiconductor players in Malaysia — including Renesas Electronics, ROHM Co Ltd, Murata Manufacturing, Toshiba Corporation, Shin-Etsu Chemical and Sumitomo Electric Industries — support the chip ecosystem, particularly in specialised semiconductor components, he said.
Kuremura added that Malaysia is Japan's fourth-largest investment destination within the Asean region and serves as a key base for its semiconductor investments in the region.
"Singapore remains the top investment destination in Asean, mainly driven by the finance sector, while Thailand and Indonesia rank second and third, respectively, with investments largely concentrated in the automotive sector," he said.
Kuremura said that Japan is also providing a matching platform to promote investment collaboration for start-ups in Japan and Asean.
"Within the Asean region, Japan is eyeing collaboration mainly in four pillars, namely boosting supply chain resiliency, innovation, digital and energy transition.
"Japan played a major role in the Asean region through its projects in the Asia Zero Emission Community (AZEC), promoting energy transition from fossil fuel towards decarbonisation in Asia," he said.
Japan is one of the AZEC partner countries, which include Australia, Brunei, Cambodia, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, the Philippines, Singapore and Thailand.
Another potential investment between Japan and Asean is in the critical rare earth supply that is used as an advanced material in the automotive and aerospace industries.
In the area of computing resources, Kuremura said Japan would like to share its technology that has been developed in Kyushu, which is termed the Silicon Island of Japan, for Asean companies.
"Big chip manufacturers such as Japan Advanced Semiconductor Manufacturing (JASM) and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSMC) could provide the infrastructure to promote AI and advanced computing in Asean. This Taiwan company has anchored the semiconductor hub in Kyushu," he said.
According to the Department of Statistics Malaysia, Japan is among the top three countries for foreign direct investment in the fourth quarter of 2025, with RM103.8 billion registered.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia, China seen as Asia's more resilient economies amid energy market volatility
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia and China are emerging as relatively resilient economies in Asia amid intensifying global energy market volatility due to the ongoing conflict in Middle East.
JP Morgan head of Asia and co-head of global emerging markets equity strategy, Rajiv Batra, told CNBC in an interview that, beyond China and Malaysia, most economies in the region appear more vulnerable to energy shocks.
He said Malaysia's strength lies in its energy export profile and disciplined policy framework, which provide a buffer against external pressures.
"Malaysia's fiscal deficit was very much well under control due to government policy and inflation is not significantly high enough," he said.
These factors help support not only the country's equity markets but also its currency amid external pressures, he added.
For China, Rajiv noted that the country relies on imports for only five per cent of its electricity generation, with the bulk of electricity generated domestically.
In addition, China maintains substantial strategic reserves and retains the ability to scale up alternative energy sources, including coal and renewables, if needed.
However, Rajiv warned that the broader outlook remains uncertain, and that if geopolitical tensions continue to disrupt oil and gas supply chains - particularly through infrastructure damage and logistical delays - global growth could come under pressure.
For equity markets, Rajiv said the immediate impact is expected in energy-sensitive sectors such as consumer goods, utilities, and downstream industries.
However, he said if the situation is prolonged, it could trigger a wider impact on financials, technology, telecommunications, and even healthcare.
Despite these risks, Rajiv noted that markets have yet to fully price in a worst-case scenario and are still pricing a "muddle-through" scenario at this juncture.
–Bernama
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Kedah to extend PdPR if hot weather continues
Article:
ALOR STAR: The implementation of Home-Based Teaching and Learning (PdPR) in areas recording Level 2 daily temperatures in Kedah will be extended if the hot weather persists.
Kedah Education director Abdul Rahim Mat said the department is closely monitoring daily temperature readings issued by the Malaysian Meteorological Department (MetMalaysia) before making any decisions.
He said PdPR is being conducted in Kota Star and Pendang from today until next Tuesday after both districts recorded Level 2 daily temperatures for three consecutive days.
A total of 86,030 students were involved, comprising 65,634 students from 154 schools in Kota Star and 20,396 students from 52 schools in Pendang.
"The implementation of PdPR is in line with the ministry's guidelines on the operation of educational institutions during hot weather.
"Current temperatures have reached 37-degree Celsius and are extremely high.
"The state education department decided to implement PdPR to minimise student movement so they are not exposed to the heat, especially during peak midday hours," he told reporters after visiting Sekolah Menengah Kebangsaan Sultanah Asma here today.
Abdul Rahim said the hot weather has not disrupted teaching and learning sessions, which are continuing as planned.
He said the department, schools and teachers had been preparing since last Wednesday following an early warning on the possibility of school closures due to extreme heat.
"If temperature readings over the next three days remain the same, PdPR will continue.
"However, if temperatures decrease, face-to-face schooling will resume as normal.
"We want to ensure the safety and health of students, as hot weather can lead to various health complications.
"As such, we urge parents and guardians to ensure their children's well-being, including limiting prolonged exposure to hot weather," he said.
Meanwhile, Abdul Rahim said the department is aware of device and internet connectivity issues faced by some students.
He said schools are providing worksheets to ensure that the syllabus planned earlier can be completed.
Meanwhile, Sekolah Menengah Kebangsaan Sultanah Asma principal Tengku Fariza Tengku Ibrahim said 201 out of 217 boarding students had returned for face-to-face learning, while the rest are undergoing PdPR from today.
"For Form One to Form Three students, PdPR sessions run from 8am to 1pm, while Form Four and Form Five sessions are from 8am to 2pm.
"The PdPR schedule differs, with classes conducted for one hour followed by a 15-minute break before resuming," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 5 things you didn't know about iPhone
Article:
PARIS:  iPhone maker Apple celebrates its 50th anniversary on April 1, having marked pop culture and the tech industry like few other firms since its beginnings in 1976. Here are five things you may not know about the history of the California giant.
APPLE LOGO
Designer Rob Janoff said Apple co-founder Steve Jobs gave him one terse instruction when he commissioned a new logo in January 1977: "don't make it cute". He included the bite mark for scale to set the apple apart from similar round fruit like cherries, learning only later it was a homonym for the computer term "byte".
'1984' ADVERTISEMENT
In a totalitarian sci-fi world, a hammer thrown by a young athlete smashes a "Big Brother" figure declaiming to brainwashed citizens from a vast screen.
Tens of millions of Americans saw director Ridley Scott's one-minute Apple advertisement during the Super Bowl on Jan 22, 1984. Broadcast with an announcement of the release of the Apple computer, it was more than a little inspired by George Orwell's  novel 1984.
BOLD COLOURS
Apple's devices have over the years played with colour to set themselves apart from more staid competitors.
09:41 PHOTOS
Anyone who has watched more than one Apple product announcement or browsed its website will see a remarkable coincidence: almost every screen appears to show the time as 9:41 am.
Scott Forstall, then-head of Apple's mobile operating system iOS,  explained that the timing was down to Jobs' preferred structure for announcements.
APPLE'S THIRD MAN
Jobs and Steve Wozniak have gone down in history as Apple co-founders. In fact, a third man also signed the three-page contract that launched the company on April 1, 1976: Ronald Wayne. But just 11 days later, he sold his 10 per cent stake for two instalments of US$800 and US$1,500. That 10 per cent share would have been worth around US$370 billion by 2026.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: No need for PdPR in Terengganu for now
Article:
KUALA NERUS: There is no need to implement home-based teaching and learning (PdPR) in the state for now.
Terengganu Education director Kamsah @ Kamal Mohamed said the state is not listed among those at very high risk of experiencing extreme heat.
"We will always heed the advice of the Malaysian Meteorological Department (MetMalaysia) and the Health Ministry, as well as the Education Ministry, to take action if we face extreme heat conditions.
"The authority to implement PdPR or decide on school uniform policies lies with the schools and the department.
"The department will also monitor the situation, while schools are also given the same mandate," he said when met after conducting a visit to SK Bukit Tumbuh here today.
He said if weather conditions become excessively hot, the department will authorise temporary school closures, during which PdPR will be implemented.
"For now, there is no need for PdPR as the learning process still requires students to attend school. InsyaAllah, the situation remains safe for now.
"The highest temperature recorded so far is 33°C, and if it reaches 35°C, all outdoor activities must be halted," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Indian tile makers feel heat of Mideast war energy crunch
Article:
MORBI: Blazing-hot kilns in India's US$6.5 billion ceramics manufacturing hub employing hundreds of thousands of people have gone cold, shut down in an energy crunch caused by the Middle East war.
At a cavernous facility in Morbi, in India's western Gujarat state, a 200-metre-long propane-powered kiln that normally fires clay nonstop is silent. It is one of hundreds of plants supplying homeware tiles across the world that have been forced to suspend operations.
Thick layers of dust smother giant grinding and pressing machines, while only a tiny crew of workers transfer the last batch made three weeks ago off snaking assembly lines and into trucks.
"We are suffering a lot," said Kishor Dulera, a tile unit proprietor who closed this factory and two others in early March, sending hundreds of workers home.
India, the world's fourth-largest economy, depends on imports for 60 per cent of its liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) demand.
The overwhelming majority comes through the Strait of Hormuz waterway, which has been effectively blocked by Iran after US-Israeli strikes on the Islamic republic late last month sparked a broader regional conflict, disrupting global energy supplies.
New Delhi has prioritised supply for households, shielding Indians who use it to cook food. That has squeezed the amount available for industrial use.
Factories in sectors including stainless steel and plastic have cut back on production as a result.
'
DISASTER'
"If the main raw material is disturbed, the whole industry is disturbed," Dulera told AFP.
The ceramics hub at Morbi makes up 90 per cent of India's total production and is one of the world's largest ceramic manufacturing centres, exporting tiles to countries like the United States and Thailand.
The industry at Morbi provides direct and indirect employment to nearly a million people, according to government statistics.
More than 400 plants have closed after the gas supply chain was "broken", said Manoj Arvadiya, head of the local manufacturing industry association.
Keeping the kilns hot means plants operate around-the-clock -- and emergency shutdowns can damage machinery.
"It is a continuous process," said Arvadiya.
"You can't run it for two days and then switch it off for one day. It doesn't work like that."
Hitesh Detroja said his now-shut Lexus Granito plant had produced 30,000 tiles per day, calling the closure a "disaster". He said he has no income to pay his fixed costs and interest on loans of US$74,000 each month.
"This crisis is horrible," Detroja said.
'WORRIED'
The ripple effects of the energy crisis have been felt throughout the Morbi ecosystem.
"Everyone is worried," said 29-year-old labourer Bunty Goswami, a migrant worker at a shut plant.
"We are confused about what to do -- whether we should go home, or not."
India's government has boosted domestic LPG production, negotiated the passage of a handful of tankers and purchased new cargoes from Australia and Russia.
That should, in time, ease supply constraints.
Jitendra Aghara of Simpolo Tiles, one of Morbi's biggest manufacturers, has kept operating by buying propane at more than double the price it was before the war to ensure he fulfills customer orders.
"If now we suffer a loss for two to three months, in the future we will get it back," he said.
The tile industry developed in Gujarat due to the availability of clay and good transport connections, but it relies on overseas energy to fire.
Aghara said the previous abundance of LPG and natural gas meant that there had been few moves to fully develop and adopt electric or hydrogen-powered kilns.
Indian corporate giants like Reliance Industries have big green hydrogen production plans, which could remove Morbi's import dependencies.
But first, development is needed to ensure hybrid kilns can produce tiles of the quality that Morbi is known for.
Aghara noted alternative fuels can't replace gas "100 per cent just yet".
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Traders all smiles as Kuantan heatwave sends watermelon sales soaring
Article:
KUANTAN: The heatwave that has affected the district over the past few weeks has turned out to be a blessing for watermelon traders operating along Jalan Kuantan–Pekan near Kampung Ubai here.
Sales of the juicy fruit have been soaring, with prices now ranging between RM2 and RM3 per kg, compared with just RM1 per kg early last month.
Farmer Husaini Abdullah, 49, said he sells between 300 and 400kg of watermelon daily, with a steady stream of customers frequenting his stall in recent days.
The 49-year-old, who operates a 1.1ha farm in Kampung Ubai, said the boci (seedless) variety of watermelon has been a favourite among customers.
"The fruit is tastier and sweeter, even though its skin colour is less attractive. The average size is between 12kg and 13kg each, making it easy for consumers to purchase as it is not too heavy.
"Although this season's production is high, it has not resulted in a significant drop in prices compared with last year," he said when met today.
Husaini said farmers in Kampung Ubai cultivate their watermelons in two phases, with the first harvest at the beginning of Ramadan and the second after Aidilfitri.
He said that since the hot weather hit the district, demand for watermelons has increased fourfold, with road users travelling along the Kuantan–Pekan dual carriageway to return to their hometowns for Aidilfitri stopping to purchase the fruit.
Apart from operating his stall under a canopy along Jalan Kuantan–Pekan, Husaini also sells his produce to wholesalers and the Federal Agricultural Marketing Authority (Fama).
Meanwhile, Abdul Rahmani Khadri, 34, said the hot season has boosted income for watermelon farmers and traders in the area.
"We did not expect the hot and dry weather to arrive earlier than in previous years.
"Concerns about a drop in watermelon prices due to oversupply have proven to be untrue.
"Farmers are grateful as demand for the fruit continues to rise, with prices remaining at a satisfactory level," he said, adding that he has been involved in farming for more than 10 years.
An oversupply of watermelons in the area last month saw some farmers forced to sell their produce for as low as 70 sen per kg, before Fama stepped in to purchase tonnes of the fruit to help them avoid losses.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: How Malaysia can help build a smarter Global South economy
Article:
WHILE the old powers lurch between protectionism and geopolitical panic, the Global South is quietly beginning to reshape the world economy from below — not with slogans, but with ports, factories, industrial corridors and strategic investments that bind developing economies together in more productive and mutually beneficial ways.
This is where the next global system will be built. And Malaysia should not be a spectator.
Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim has rightly argued that the Global South is reclaiming "a voice that can no longer be ignored in the emerging international order."
But that voice cannot rest on rhetoric alone. If Malaysia wants to lead, it must do more than speak about South-South cooperation. It must help build it through concrete, strategic business partnerships that deepen industrial capacity across the developing world.
A powerful example already exists. In San Pedro, Ivory Coast, Malaysian cocoa giant Guan Chong has shown what intelligent South-South investment can look like.
By investing in cocoa grinding capacity close to one of the world's richest cocoa-producing zones, it has done more than expand its own global footprint. It has helped shift value-added activity closer to the source of production.
Ivory Coast gains processing jobs, technical capability and a greater share of the value chain. Guan Chong gains stronger supply access, logistical efficiency and a more strategic position in world markets.
That is not charity. It is not extraction. It is smart industrial partnership.
And it offers a template for what Malaysia can do next — especially in two sectors where we possess world-class capabilities: palm oil and rubber.
West Africa is the natural frontier. Take palm oil. The historical irony is striking: oil palm is native to West Africa.
In the 1960s, Malaysia and Ivory Coast were starting from roughly the same level in palm oil production. Yet today Malaysia produces 40 times more output.
This is one of the great postcolonial development divergences. The difference was not geography. It was institutions, organisation, research, processing capacity and the ability to turn a crop into a complete industrial ecosystem.
That ecosystem is now one of Malaysia's great economic strengths. Palm oil is not merely an export commodity; it is a pillar of the national economy.
In 2024, palm oil contributed RM114 billion in export value and 2.3 per cent of GDP. Malaysia is not simply a successful producer.
It is a world leader in certified sustainable palm oil, with decades of accumulated expertise in planting materials, estate management, milling, refining, logistics and downstream processing.
But at home, the sector faces structural challenges: land constraints, ageing estates, labour pressures and rising costs.
West Africa offers what Malaysia increasingly lacks — room to expand, ecological suitability and proximity to fast-growing regional markets.
The opportunity, however, is not simply to plant more oil palm. That would be the old colonial model: acquire land, grow the crop, export the output.
Malaysia should reject that approach. Our real advantage is not just capital. It is the ability to build systems.
Malaysia's palm oil success was built not only on plantations, but also on research, extension services, integrated mills, refineries, logistics and the organisation of smallholders into productive value chains.
That is the real lesson of the Felda era: when smallholders are properly supported, productivity rises, incomes improve and rural transformation becomes possible.
This is precisely what much of West Africa needs.
Countries such as Ivory Coast have land, farmers and market potential. What is often missing is the institutional architecture: better seedlings, agronomic support, aggregation systems, milling capacity, refining and downstream industries.
Malaysian firms can help fill that gap — not by displacing local farmers, but by organising them into more productive systems, raising yields on existing land and building industries in edible oils, oleochemicals and consumer products.
In other words, Malaysia's most valuable export may not be palm oil itself. It may be institutional know-how. The same logic applies, perhaps even more powerfully, to rubber.
Malaysia is one of the world's clearest examples of how a commodity economy can move up the value chain. Over decades, it built a globally dominant latex-based manufacturing sector, especially in rubber gloves.
Even after the pandemic boom subsided, Malaysia remains the world leader in natural rubber gloves. This year, natural rubber glove exports were worth RM15.57 billion, accounting for 46 per cent of total rubber-based exports.
That is a remarkable industrial achievement. It shows how Malaysia transformed upstream agricultural production into sophisticated downstream manufacturing.
West Africa can benefit from that same playbook.
Countries such as Ivory Coast, Liberia and Ghana already have a rubber base. But, as with palm oil, the issue is not whether rubber can be grown.
It is whether yields can be raised, latex collection improved, processing upgraded and local manufacturing deepened.
Malaysian expertise in planting material, replanting systems, latex collection, grading, aggregation and processing could help transform the sector.
If that happens, West African economies gain far more than raw commodity exports. They gain jobs, technical capability, industrial upgrading and a larger share of the value created from their own resources.
And Malaysia gains too. A stronger upstream rubber partnership in West Africa can make Malaysian rubber product companies, from gloves to industrial rubber goods and automotive components, more resilient and more globally competitive.
In an age of supply-chain disruption and geopolitical fragmentation, that is not a side benefit. It is a strategic advantage. This is the larger point.
The old model of globalisation is breaking down. For decades, the South supplied raw materials while the North captured the margins. That system was unequal, unstable and ultimately unsustainable.
But the answer cannot be Trump-style economic nationalism — a world of tariffs, coercion and every country for itself. Nor can it be nostalgia for an old international order that never truly served most developing countries.
What we need instead is a new model of sustainable globalisation: one in which the Global South invests in the Global South, builds productive capacity in the Global South, processes more of its own resources and shares value more equitably across borders.
Malaysia is unusually well placed to help lead that shift. Cocoa in San Pedro is the proof of concept. Palm oil in Ivory Coast could be the next frontier.
Rubber across West Africa could be the industrial multiplier. Together, they point to something larger than any single sector: a Malaysian-led template for a more mature, more strategic and more mutually beneficial Global South partnership.
Not a scramble for land or a new commodity frontier, or a softer version of the old extractive model.
But a partnership built on institutional transfer, industrial upgrading, stronger smallholder livelihoods, deeper local value addition and shared long-term competitiveness. In short, putting into practice the spirit of Bandung 55'.
While Trump tears down the old international system, Malaysia and the Global South should be busy building the new one. And Malaysia should not merely join that future. It should take the lead to design it.
* The writer is chairman of Citizen's International, a civil society organisation based in Penang dedicated to the pursuit of peaceful coexistence and global cooperation in a just world. https://www.citizens-international.org
The views expressed in this article are the author's own and do not necessarily reflect those of the New Straits Times
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Poultry firm fined RM30,000 over illegal effluent discharge in Kedah
Article:
ALOR SETAR: A chicken slaughtering company in Bukit Pinang has been fined RM30,000 by the Sessions Court for failing to comply with industrial effluent discharge standards.
The Kedah Department of Environment (DOE) said the company pleaded guilty to two charges under the Environmental Quality Act 1974.
Its director, Sharifah Zakiah Syed Sahab, said the offences involved the discharge of effluent exceeding permitted limits.
The first charge related to effluent containing excessive levels of biochemical oxygen demand (BOD5 at 20°C), suspended solids (SS) and ammonia nitrogen (AN), breaching Standard B under the Fifth Schedule.
The second charge involved chemical oxygen demand (COD) levels exceeding limits set under Standard B of the Seventh Schedule.
The offences were detected during an integrated enforcement operation on 22 July last year, when DOE officers collected samples from the company's final discharge point at its industrial effluent treatment system.
The samples were later analysed by the Chemistry Department Malaysia, confirming breaches of environmental regulations.
Sessions Court judge N. Priscilla Hemamalini imposed the fine after the company's director, representing the firm, pleaded guilty to both charges.
The company was charged under Regulations 11(1)(b) and 12(b) of the Environmental (Industrial Effluent) Regulations 2009, which carry penalties under Regulation 32.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 78 litterbugs ordered to perform community service across six states
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: A total of 78 litterbugs were ordered to undergo Community Service Orders (CSO) yesterday for littering offences under the Solid Waste and Public Cleansing Management Act.
The community service was carried out simultaneously across six states — Perlis, Kedah, the Federal Territories, Pahang, Negri Sembilan and Johor.
Housing and Local Government Minister Nga Kor Ming said the enforcement effort was in line with the government's vision of "Clean Malaysia, Prosperous People", and formed part of an integrated strategy to curb indiscriminate littering in public spaces and along roads.
He said if left unchecked, the habit of littering could lead to larger problems such as clogged drains, which would affect public comfort and contribute to flash floods in urban areas.
"The government aims to educate the public to be more responsible in safeguarding environmental cleanliness collectively.
"Do not throw cigarette butts, tissues or drink cans indiscriminately, as this not only pollutes the environment but also tarnishes the country's image," he said.
The community service orders focused on comprehensive cleaning works, including pavements, public roads, drainage systems (scupper drains), public toilets and coastal areas.
The initiative was in line with the government's aspiration to cultivate sustainable solid waste management practices.
To date, the Solid Waste Management and Public Cleansing Corporation (SWCorp) has issued 1,342 Notices of Offence (NPK) across seven states under the Solid Waste and Public Cleansing Management Act, involving 1,058 Malaysians and 284 foreigners.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: AmInvestment keeps 'Buy' on Kim Loong despite Q4 earnings dip
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Kim Loong Resources Bhd saw higher wage costs in the fourth quarter (Q4), mainly due to the increase in minimum wage and Employees Provident Fund contributions for foreign workers.
AmInvestment Bank Bhd said the group's earnings fell 17.9 per cent quarter-on-quarter in Q4, weighed down by lower palm product prices as well as higher wages and transportation costs.
Transportation costs rose following restrictions on truck loading limits.
The research house noted that milling earnings rose 4.1 per cent to RM122.2 million in the financial year ended Jan 31, 2026 (FY26), supported by a 6.9 per cent increase in fresh fruit bunch (FFB) processed to 1.6 million tonnes.
On a quarterly basis, milling earnings improved to RM80 per tonne in Q4 from RM66 per tonne in the preceding quarter, as FFB processed increased by 13 per cent.
"Going forward, we expect milling profit margins to be sustained. We believe Kim Loong Resources will raise milling charges in FY27 to pass on higher transportation and compliance costs to customers," it said.
AmInvestment Bank maintained its "Buy" call on the stock with a target price of RM3, adding that the group is likely to benefit from firmer crude palm oil prices.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Family of 11 escapes blaze that engulfs Ulu Yam home
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Eleven people managed to escape before a fire engulfed a single-storey family home in Kampung Pasir, Ulu Yam Baru, late last night.
Selangor Fire and Rescue Department assistant director (operations) Ahmad Mukhlis Mukhtar said the department received a distress call at 11.21pm and dispatched personnel from the Kuala Kubu Baru station, with assistance from Bukit Sentosa and Rawang.
"Fire engines arrived at the scene at 11.42pm, about 16km from the station, and brought the blaze under control at 12.13am.
"The fire involved a Class A structure measuring approximately 60 by 50sq ft, with about 80 per cent of the house destroyed," he said in a statement.
Mukhlis said the victims were three men, four women, including one with disabilities, a boy and three girls.
He said 14 personnel were involved in the operation, supported by two fire rescue tenders and a water tanker.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Open burning drives 1,418 fire incidents in Kelantan
Article:
Sharifah Mahsinah Abdullah
KOTA BARU: A total of 1,418 fire incidents were recorded in Kelantan between Jan 1 and March 29 this year, with the majority involving open burning.
Kelantan Fire and Rescue Department director Farhan Sufyan Borhan said 1,089 cases were classified as open burning, affecting an estimated 1,620ha statewide.
"Of the total, 880 cases involved bush fires, 114 forest fires, 70 rubbish fires and 25 plantation fires," he said.
Farhan said Kota Baru recorded the highest number of open burning cases at 400, followed by Bachok (278) and Pasir Mas (173).
He said the department had mobilised its assets to combat the situation, including 28 fire engines, seven water tankers with capacities of 10,000 litres and 20,000 litres, as well as a Hydro-Sub vehicle across 22 fire stations statewide.
"Continuous monitoring operations were also carried out, particularly in peatland areas estimated at 7,689ha, mostly located in Bachok, Tumpat, Pasir Puteh, Tok Bali and Pasir Mas," he said.
Farhan said there was a sharp increase in open burning incidents in March, accounting for 64 per cent of total calls received so far this year.
He said the department expected the number to rise further in line with forecasts by the Meteorological Department, which predicted dry weather conditions until mid-April, possibly extending to June.
To curb the situation, he said the department had been sharing information with local authorities and the Department of Environment to enable enforcement under the Local Government Act 1976 and the Environmental Quality Act 1974.
"Offenders and landowners can face fines, imprisonment or compounds if found responsible for open burning," he said.
Despite the surge, he said the overall situation remained under control, although the department faced challenges such as limited water sources and difficult access to fire locations.
"Should the situation worsen, we will implement a targeted leave freeze and deploy aerial support using helicopters from the Fire and Rescue Department's Air Unit to ensure smoother operations," he said.
He urged the public to remain vigilant and refrain from open burning to prevent the situation from escalating.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Overloaded passenger boat detained off Pulau Redang
Article:
KUALA NERUS: The Malaysian Maritime Enforcement Agency (MMEA) detained a passenger boat found to be carrying more than the permitted number of passengers during a patrol operation in the waters off Pulau Redang yesterday.
Terengganu MMEA director Maritime Captain Hamiludin Che Awang said the boat was detained at about 3 pm, some 26 nautical miles from the mouth of Kuala Terengganu, after it was spotted carrying a large number of passengers.
"An inspection found that the boat was carrying 25 passengers, compared with its licensed capacity of only 12 as stipulated in its valid licence," he said.
He said the situation posed a safety risk to all passengers in the event of an emergency at sea.
He said the boat was operated by three crew members aged between 19 and 23, and some of them were also not wearing life jackets.
All passengers were safely disembarked, while the boat and its crew were detained and taken to the Terengganu MMEA jetty for further investigation under the Merchant Shipping Ordinance 1952 for breaching licence conditions. – BERNAMA
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia seeks Egypt's help to deliver 374 tonnes of aid to Gaza
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia has sought Egypt's help to deliver 374 tonnes of humanitarian aid to Gaza via the Rafah border crossing starting this April.
Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim said this was conveyed during his recent interaction with Egyptian President Abdel Fattah El-Sisi.
"I asked for his cooperation to deliver 374 tonnes of Malaysian humanitarian aid to Gaza via the Rafah border crossing starting this April."
"Alhamdulillah, President El-Sisi assured me that access would be eased, allowing aid to reach more than 100,000 Gazans," he said in a Facebook post today.
Anwar said Malaysia would not bow to pressure, especially from Israel, which he said continues to hinder humanitarian efforts.
"At a time of global indifference and hypocrisy, Malaysia will continue to stand as a principled voice, defending the rights of the oppressed."
"I pray that all our efforts will be eased and that this aid will reach our brothers and sisters in Gaza safely, God willing."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: 108,000 litres dropped as Rompin plantation fire enters seventh day
Article:
ROMPIN: An MI-17 helicopter has been deployed to carry out water-bombing operations as a fire at an oil palm plantation in Kampung Jemeri entered its seventh day.
The Pahang Fire and Rescue Department said 135 water-bombing runs were carried out at several hotspots from yesterday to noon today.
Its public relations officer, Zulfadli Zakaria, said 68,000 litres of water were dropped yesterday, with a further 40,000 litres released by midday today.
"The operation involves eight crew members from the department's air unit, including two officers," he said in a statement.
He said about 300 hectares of a 1,214-hectare plantation had been affected, with 59 hectares successfully extinguished so far, including 19 hectares yesterday.
Firefighting efforts are ongoing, with the air unit working alongside 50 personnel from seven fire stations across Pahang to contain the remaining 240 hectares.
They are supported by 10 members of the Civil Defence Force and five plantation workers.
In a separate incident, a plantation fire in Nenasi, Pekan — now in its eighth day — has affected about 735 hectares of a 2,000-hectare area.
Of that, 520 hectares have been extinguished, including around 200 hectares yesterday, leaving 196 hectares still burning.
A total of 34 firefighters from 10 stations have been deployed to assist personnel at the Nenasi fire station.
Zulfadli added that firefighters are also dealing with two separate landfill fires in Gebeng, as
multiple large-scale blazes continue across the state.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Traffic slows on major highways as return travel to Kuala Lumpur continues
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Traffic was slow and congested on several major highways nationwide as of midday today, as return travel to the capital continued.
Updates from the East Coast Expressway Phase 1 showed congestion on westbound routes between Bentong and Lentang, as well as around Bukit Tinggi, with traffic building up over stretches of between 800m and 1.4km.
Affected sections included KM58.2 to KM56.8, KM55.8 to KM54.9 and KM52.2 to KM51 near Lentang, as well as KM49.7 to KM48.9 in Bukit Tinggi.
Meanwhile, PLUS Malaysia Berhad reported slow traffic along several stretches of the North-South Expressway and connected routes in both directions.
Southbound congestion was recorded from Sungai Perak rest and service area to the Menora Tunnel, Permatang Pauh to Prai, Simpang Renggam to Sedenak, and Yong Peng to Ayer Hitam.
Northbound delays were reported from Putra Mahkota to Southville City, and from Bandar Ainsdale to Bandar Baru Nilai.
Traffic was also slow at other locations, including from Sungkai to Slim River and on approaches to the Menora Tunnel heading towards Ipoh.
In the Klang Valley, congestion was observed on the Elite Expressway northbound from Putrajaya to Bandar Saujana Putra, as well as after the Sungai Besi toll plaza heading towards the city centre.
Motorists have been advised to drive cautiously and stay updated on current traffic conditions.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Germany's WWII munitions a toxic legacy on Baltic Sea floor
Article:
KIEL, (Germany): Below the waves off Germany's northern tourist beaches, a toxic time bomb lurks on the Baltic Sea floor – enormous quantities of World War II munitions that are slowly rusting away.
Scientists warn that as salt water corrodes the metal casings on rockets, artillery shells and bombs, they will release contaminants such as the explosive TNT into the marine environment.
To better map the dangers, a research vessel set sail this month from the port city of Kiel, whose bay is among the most polluted with unexploded ordnance.
A dozen scientists from Germany, Poland and Lithuania, backed by an 11-strong crew, are to spend three weeks on the Alkor, operated by the Kiel-based GEOMAR oceanographic research centre.
The voyage will take them past a sunken torpedo boat, a destroyer, a minesweeper and a submarine, all identified from naval logbooks and other records in the German military archives.
"One of the goals of the project is to develop some new tools for cleaning it up," Aaron Beck, a scientist leading the expedition, told AFP aboard the ship.
"The idea is, what can we do to prevent this before the pollution comes out?"
Along the German coast, about 1.6 million tonnes of munitions litter the seafloor, especially near the ports of Kiel and Luebeck, making it one of the world's most contaminated areas.
Most munitions were hastily dumped there by the victorious Allied powers after Germany's 1945 surrender, to quickly eliminate what remained of the Nazi war machine.
- Traces found in shellfish -
Almost 80 years on, traces of carcinogenic explosives have been detected in shellfish and other sea life throughout the area.
The Baltic is shallow, with only a narrow passage between Sweden and Denmark leading to the open ocean, meaning pollution tends to linger.
A modern-day boom in undersea construction of pipelines, telecom cables and offshore wind farms has cast a new spotlight on the issue.
The scientists on the ship are using an underwater robot to film the seabed, as well as probes to collect sediment and water samples.
They are also dispersing packets of mussels, which they will later retrieve to study the levels of contamination ingested.
Beck, however, reassured that the pollution does not pose an immediate danger to humans.
"For a human being to ingest, at current concentrations, a concerning amount of explosive compounds, they would have to consume seven kilos (15 pounds) of fish a day for more than a year," he said.
Ammunition on the sunken warships is not the only environmental danger.
"On some of these ships, you have 10 tonnes of ammunition, but 200 tonnes of fuel. That's undoubtedly the biggest problem," Beck said.
One wreck still holding fuel is the Franken, a German navy tanker torpedoed by Soviet forces on April 8, 1945. It sank off what is now the Polish city of Gdansk, at the time still the German city of Danzig.
Uwe Wiechert, 70, a former German naval officer and part of the research team, called it a "time bomb."
The Franken also poses a legal conundrum, he said: who will pay to pump this fuel from a German ship, sunk by the Soviets, that now rests in Polish waters?
- Slow disposal efforts -
Seafloor munitions dumps are a global problem, with other major sites located along the coasts of the United States, Britain, Japan and Australia and even in Swiss lakes.
Germany has been at the forefront of European efforts to deal with unexploded underwater ordnance, says the European Commission.
Beyond mapping the problem, Germany has taken first steps toward munitions disposal.
In Luebeck Bay, a pilot project to destroy WWII munitions on a specially built floating disposal platform has begun.
Some contractors working on the project have experience of clearing munitions for large offshore wind farms along the Baltic and North Sea coasts.
Divers and underwater robots have sorted through tonnes of dumped munitions at four sites in the bay as part of the project, funded with an initial 100 million euros (RM462 million).
But it remains unclear whether the pilot project could become a model for cleanups elsewhere.
So far, at least, no government has committed the long-term funding needed to tackle the problem.
When a similar project might start in waters off Kiel, said Beck, "is anybody's guess." — AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Concerns grow over aggressive 'freelance photographers' at KLCC
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Concerns are mounting over the behaviour of unlicensed photographers operating around the Petronas Twin Towers, with visitors reporting aggressive tactics and growing unease at one of the city's busiest tourist spots.
Groups of men offering paid photography services — often approaching both local and foreign visitors — have been active in the area, particularly in the evenings, prompting questions over enforcement and public safety.
Some visitors said the photographers compete for customers, at times creating a tense atmosphere.
Che Mohd Azuan Che Awang, 41, who has lived in Kuala Lumpur for 16 years, said the situation had worsened over time.
"I have been approached multiple times, but I declined. For those unfamiliar with the area, their presence can be worrying," he told Berita Harian.
"The same photos can be taken on our own, so people may feel uncomfortable being repeatedly approached," he added, calling for more visible enforcement in the area.
A visit to the site yesterday found groups of photographers continuing to operate, particularly near bus waiting areas around KLCC.
The issue has also drawn official attention. Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Federal Territories) Hannah Yeoh said more closed-circuit television (CCTV) cameras would be installed along Persiaran KLCC and Jalan Ampang to curb illegal photography activities.
She said discussions would be held with KLCC Property Holdings Berhad and Kuala Lumpur City Hall (DBKL) to increase surveillance in the high-traffic tourist area.
The move follows a recent incident in which 18 men, aged between 17 and 45, were arrested after allegedly assaulting a man who intervened in a dispute involving a foreign tourist.
According to Dang Wangi police chief Assistant Commissioner Sazalee Adam, the incident began when a group of photographers attacked an Indian tourist about 9pm on March 22.
A local man who attempted to defuse the situation was also assaulted, reportedly with a weapon.
Foreign visitors say such services are common globally but should be conducted transparently.
Tomas Azevedo, a 34-year-old tourist from Brazil, said photographers should clearly explain their services and obtain consent to avoid misunderstandings.
"This is my first time in Malaysia, and KLCC is a beautiful public space," he said.
"I hope the authorities can monitor the situation so visitors can enjoy the area without disturbance."
Local visitor Irdina Ibrahim, 19, said the presence of the photographers affected her sense of safety.
"It feels uncomfortable, especially for young people who just want to relax or shop here," she said.
She called for stricter enforcement and increased security presence in the area.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Domestic Trade Ministry seizes RM32m in petrol and diesel, arrest 667 under Ops Tiris 3.0
Article:
BUKIT KAYU HITAM, March 29 — The Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry (KPDN) seized petrol and diesel worth more than RM32 million and arrested 667 people under the Ops Tiris 3.0 conducted nationwide from January 1, 2024 to March 27 this year.
KPDN deputy director-general of enforcement (operations) Shamsul Nizam Khalil said that during the period, 38,615 inspections were carried out and 1,371 cases were recorded.
“KPDN will continue to intensify integrated operations to curb any leakage, smuggling and misappropriation of petrol and diesel.
“This effort is strengthened through strategic collaboration with other enforcement agencies, including the Royal Malaysia Police, Malaysian Border Control and Protection Agency, Road Transport Department, Immigration Department and the Royal Malaysian Customs Department,” he told reporters here today.
He said inter-agency cooperation would enable the authorities to further tighten controls at all national entry and exit points to prevent the smuggling of these commodities.
He added that intelligence gathering, inspections and intensive monitoring are also being carried out at petrol stations and in identified hotspots.
He warned individuals and syndicates not to take advantage of the current conflict in West Asia.
“KPDN will ensure the stability of supply of controlled goods, namely petrol and diesel, in the market despite geopolitical uncertainties arising from the escalating conflict in West Asia.
“KPDN has formulated aggressive strategies to address any potential disruptions to global fuel supplies that could impact the national economy,” he said. — Bernama

Title: MetMalaysia issues thunderstorm and strong‑wind alert for multiple states until 5pm
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 29 — The Malaysian Meteorological Department (MetMalaysia) has issued a warning of thunderstorms, heavy rain and strong winds in several areas in the Peninsula, Sarawak and Sabah until 5pm today.
MetMalaysia said the states expected to be affected in the Peninsula are Penang (Southwest, Northeast and Seberang Perai Utara); Perak (Batang Padang and Muallim); Pahang (Cameron Highlands, Raub and Rompin); and Johor (Segamat, Kluang, Kulai, Kota Tinggi and Johor Bahru).
In Sarawak, the areas set to be affected are Kuching, Serian, Samarahan, Sri Aman, Betong (Saratok and Kabong), Sarikei, Sibu, Mukah (Tanjung Manis, Daro and Matu), Kapit, Bintulu (Tatau) and Limbang.
In Sabah, the areas likely to be hit are the interior regions (Sipitang, Tenom, Beaufort and Nabawan), the West Coast (Papar, Putatan, Penampang, Kota Kinabalu, Tuaran and Kota Belud) and Tawau (Tawau and Lahad Datu). — Bernama

Title: Police open murder probe after discovery of charred body, burnt motorcycle in Kulim
Article:
KULIM, March 29 — Police have opened a murder investigation following the discovery of a charred body alongside a burnt motorcycle at Ladang Anak Kulim yesterday.
Kulim police chief Zulkifli Azizan said the Terap Police Station received a call at 2.25 pm from a member of the public who stumbled upon the body while in the area.
“The individual had gone to the location to catch birds and had stumbled upon the burnt body and motorcycle. He then left the area and lodged a police report,” he said in a statement.
Based on the motorcycle’s registration, police managed to trace the victim’s next of kin, who reported that the 77-year-old man had been missing for four days.
Police are investigating the case under Section 302 of the Penal Code for murder. Two men, aged 22 and 68, have been arrested and remanded for four days until April 1 to assist in the investigation. — Bernama

Title: Malaysia to bar foreign debit and credit cards from RON95 purchases to curb subsidy misuse
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The use of foreign credit and debit cards to purchase RON95 petrol nationwide will reportedly be restricted in stages starting Wednesday.
According to
Berita Haria
n, Ministry of Domestic Trade and Cost of Living enforcement director-general Datuk Azman Adam said the move is to ensure subsidies are limited to eligible Malaysians and prevent misuse, including by foreign-registered vehicles.
“This is one of the new mechanisms to control the purchase and sale of RON95 petrol at all petrol stations in the country.
“Previously, there were many cases of vehicles with foreign registration plates refuelling using foreign debit or credit cards at self-service pumps, making it difficult for operators and authorities to detect such purchases,” he was quoted as saying.
He reportedly said foreign card users will instead be required to pay at the counter, allowing better monitoring and enforcement.
Azman was quoted as saying that the measure also helps stations with many pumps detect leakages and ensure ineligible users cannot access subsidised fuel.
He reportedly added that some oil companies are ready to implement the system, while others will do so in stages.

Title: Two men in Wangsa Maju ‘protection money’ video arrested, police probe ongoing
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR, March 29 — Two men seen in a viral video allegedly demanding protection money from a business operator have been arrested by police.
According to a report by
Malaysiakini
, Kuala Lumpur police chief Datuk Fadil Marsus confirmed the arrests last night, saying investigations are ongoing.
The two were reportedly members of the Federal Territory Residents Representative Council in Wangsa Maju and of PKR, and had been instructed to step down from their posts to facilitate investigations by both the authorities and the party.
Federal Territories PKR leadership council chairperson Azman Abidin was quoted as saying the resignations were intended to ensure transparency throughout the process.
The duo have denied the allegations, claiming their visit to a massage parlour at Wisma Setapak on March 24 was for routine monitoring purposes.
Wangsa Maju district police chief Supt Lazim Ismail said the case is being investigated under Section 385 of the Penal Code for extortion and Section 233 of the Communications and Multimedia Act 1998 for misuse of network facilities.
Earlier, a video went viral on social media showing two individuals allegedly demanding ‘protection money’ from a business premises believed to be in the capital.
Kuala Lumpur City Hall has denied any link to the alleged activities, dismissing suggestions that its officers or processes were involved.

Title: West Asia conflict may spur domestic tourism growth in Sarawak, says state minister
Article:
KUCHING, March 29 — The ongoing conflicts in West Asia are not expected to significantly impact Malaysia’s tourism sector and could even provide a boost, particularly through increased domestic travel, said Sarawak Minister of Tourism, Creative Industries and Performing Arts Abdul Karim Rahman Hamzah.
He said that global uncertainties often encourage people to travel within the country rather than abroad, indirectly supporting the local tourism industry.
“Tourism can be divided into international and domestic segments. Based on past experiences, such as during the Covid-19 pandemic,  even though borders were closed and flights were limited, Sarawak’s tourism industry remained resilient through various initiatives,” he added.
He said this during a media conference held at an Aidifitri reception hosted by the Ministry of Tourism, Creative Industries and Performing Arts (MTCP) and the Ministry of Youth, Sports and Entrepreneur Development (MySED) here today.
Abdul Karim highlighted past programmes like the Sia Sitok campaign, which successfully boosted domestic tourism by encouraging locals to explore destinations within Sarawak.
He said the state is prepared to respond to any prolonged crisis, with contingency plans such as the Post-Covid-19 Development Strategy (PCDS) 2030 already in place.
“If the situation worsens, both the Sarawak and Federal governments have plans to ease the burden on the people. There is no need to panic...we should take a positive approach in facing any challenges,” he said. — Bernama

Title: Kelantan records 1,089 open burning cases since Jan 1, says Fire Dept
Article:
KOTA BHARU, March 29 — The Kelantan Fire and Rescue Department (JBPM) has recorded 1,089 open burning cases from the overall total of 1,418 fire emergency calls received since January 1.
Its director, Farhan Sufyan Borhan, said that of the 1,089 open burning cases, 880 were bush fires, forest fires (114), garbage fires (70) and plantation fires (25), totalling 1,618 hectares statewide.
“Kota Bharu has the highest number of cases with 400, followed by Bachok (278) and Pasir Mas (173),” he said in a statement today.
To tackle these open burning cases, he said a total of 28 fire engines, seven 10,000-litre and 20,000-litre water tankers and a hydro-sub vehicle are deployed at 22 fire stations throughout the state.
“Monitoring has also been intensified, especially in the 7,689-hectare peat soil areas in Bachok, Tumpat, Pasir Puteh and Pasir Mas.
“There has been a sudden surge in calls by up to 64 per cent about open burning this month (March), which is in line with the Meteorological Department’s forecast of dry weather until mid-April or June,” he said.
He advised the public not to engage in open burning activities to prevent the situation from worsening. — Bernama

Title: Digital Ministry engages industry to improve Employment Pass processing for tech talent
Article:
PUTRAJAYA, March 29 — The Digital Ministry has assured that issues related to the processing of Employment Pass (EP) for technology talent are being actively and comprehensively scrutinised to ensure the smooth running of the Global Business Services (GBS) ecosystem.
The ministry stressed that it takes a serious view of the concerns raised by industry stakeholders, with various engagement sessions being actively implemented in collaboration with relevant agencies, including the Malaysia Digital Economy Corporation (MDEC).
“This approach aims to fully understand the concerns raised, in addition to identifying suitable short-and long-term improvement measures,” it said in a statement today.
The ministry said the GBS sector is one of the key drivers of the nation’s digital economy, contributing significantly to investment inflows, high-value job creation and the strengthening of Malaysia’s position as a competitive regional digital hub.
It added that the MDEC has intensified efforts to address the significant surge in new applications, including increasing manpower capacity to meet the growing demand.
“In addition, the MDEC is also engaging closely with key industry associations, such as GBS Malaysia (Digital Global Business Services Council Malaysia, a chapter of PIKOM) and Contact Centre Association of Malaysia (CCAM), to apprise stakeholders of the latest development,” it added.
The ministry emphasised its commitment to continue strengthening inter-agency coordination, while implementing ongoing improvement processes to further accelerate smoother approvals.
“Further updates will be provided from time to time, in line with its review and engagement with all the relevant parties,” it added. — Bernama

Title: Police arrest five men after viral car‑smashing video in Kota Kinabalu
Article:
KOTA KINABALU, March 29 — Police have detained five men over a viral video showing a group smashing a vehicle at a traffic light junction near Jesselton Quay here.
Acting Kota Kinabalu district police chief Supt Syed Lot Syed Ab Rahman said a 30-year-old teacher lodged a police report at about 2.35 pm yesterday claiming to be the victim in the incident.
He said the incident is believed to have occurred at around 1.45 pm on the same day after the teacher had attended an event at the Sabah International Convention Centre.
“Preliminary investigations found that while the complainant was heading to his vehicle in a parking area near a warehouse belonging to a courier company at Kota Kinabalu Port, he encountered several individuals believed to be employees of the company.
“A verbal provocation took place before the situation escalated, causing the complainant to reverse his vehicle and inadvertently collide with another vehicle.
“Following that, several individuals chased the complainant’s vehicle and acted aggressively by smashing the driver’s side window and the rear windscreen. The complainant also claimed he was threatened to get out of the vehicle during the incident,” he said in a statement today.
